data.title: Vicor Corporation: Great Technology, Execution Trapped In Time
data.description:
Vicor Corporation (NASDAQ:VICR) is a case study in how technical superiority doesn’t always translate to commercial execution. Two decades ahead of its time, Vicor was once the definition of high-efficiency modular power conversion. Its Factorized Power Architecture (FPA) anticipated the current trend from 12-volt to 48-volt power distribution. And yet, Vicor’s bottomline remains lackluster, and unless there’s a miracle somewhere, my calculations show that whatever potential it may have is already baked into the stock price.

Brief Overview of Vicor

Vicor is an Andover, MA-based designer and manufacturer of power conversion components. Founded in 1981 by CEO Dr. Patrizio Vinciarelli, Vicor’s core innovation is the Factorized Power Architecture (FPA), which separates regulation and transformation into two components, reducing power loss and the volume of space it takes on the board.

Vicor’s products include pre-regulator modules (PRMs), voltage transformation modules (VTMs), and newer Converter-housed-in-Package (ChiP) modules that integrate these functions into compact, surface-mountable packages. Vicor’s products have applications in aerospace, defense, data centers and AI, satellites, and automobiles. However, its superior technology has been overtaken by technology that may not be as superior, but is workable, and cheaper.

Vicor’s Patent Enforcement Strategy

Vicor is still making efforts, with novel legal strategies and some technological improvements. The company has adopted an aggressive, interesting strategy to monetize its intellectual property (IP). It is going after rivals infringing on its fixed-ratio bus converters, which are NBMs, or Non-isolated Bus Converter Modules. These NBMs lie at the core of Vicor’s technology. Their job is to take the 48V input and generate a 12V step-down at 97–98% conversion efficiency, with no galvanic isolation. That removal of unnecessary isolation improves usage of critical space, and increases efficiency. Thus, this was critical technology for Vicor’s Factorized Power Architecture (FPA).

Vicor initiated a Section 337 complaint against rivals infringing on its NBM design. This was filed with the United States International Trade Commission, and not the federal or district court system. As this was a Section 337 complaint, the defendants were mainly foreign entities or their US subsidiaries—Delta Electronics Inc. and its various subsidiaries, Hon Hai Precision Industry Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:HNHPF), commonly known as Foxconn, Quanta Computer Inc. (OTC:QUCCF), some other smaller Taiwanese companies, and their US subsidiaries.

Vicor filed the complaint in July 2023, and in February this year, the United States International Trade Commission found in their favor, confirming patent infringement occurred. The resulting order included a limited exclusion order, which limited the import of power modules and unlicensed computing systems from overseas, and a cease and desist order, which prevented the sale of these products in the US.

According to Susman Godfrey, Vicor’s legal counsel, the verdict found that “U.S. Patent Nos. 9,516,761 (“’761 patent”) and 9,166,481 (“’481 patent”) are valid and infringed” in Investigation No. 337-TA-1370. These were not monetary damages, as there could be if this were a federal or district court case; however, these rulings help Vicor protect its IP, and OEMs and hyperscalers who used the infringed products now have to approach Vicor for licensing to continue doing business using these products.

Since the ITC only issues orders but cannot award monetary damages, a parallel lawsuit was also filed in the Eastern District of Texas, and Vicor sought monetary damages from Delta, one of the alleged infringers. The case was dismissed with prejudice, meaning the parties ultimately settled. While no damages were awarded by the court, a settlement meant a monetary gain for Vicor. Notably, licensing rights were not part of this settlement. Delta instead licenses their technology to large end-user companies, the so-called hyperscalers, rather than companies that were importing systems with Vicor’s patent-protected modules.

The extent of these legal actions went beyond the US. As a related development showed, a US district court also barred Foxconn through a preliminary injunction from seeking arbitration against Vicor in China. The arbitration request sought to use the small-print boilerplate language in some of their purchase orders of Vicor products to claim “unlimited royalty-free licenses to Vicor patents.” While the outcome is still unknown, the preliminary injunction is a positive sign in Vicor's favor.

This sort of legal action is especially suitable in the current political dispensation, where there’s a strong defensive posture against real or perceived threats to US economic interests.

Licensing and Monetization Model

Along with these legal actions, Vicor has been aggressively persuading OEM manufacturers to directly license tech from Vicor instead of going through third parties. Thus, in Q4 2022, they signed their first OEM license, and this year, they signed their first hyperscaler license agreement (Google, according to Needham analysts). The company says licensing fees will bring in $200 million through 2026, with potentially another $400 million from additional licensing agreements. For a company that generated $359 million in net revenue in 2024, that is a very big windfall.

The problem with this is that these are one-time payments - or, at least, the company has avoided clarifying whether or how much of this is recurring. For example, here’s the brief exchange we had on the issue, when Richard Shannon with Craig-Hallum questioned management on the recurrent nature - or lack thereof - of the settlement/licensing fees/royalties:

…Is this settlement -- will we see any ongoing royalties from this customer? Or is it fully paid up in any manner? Can you describe who this is either by name or kind of a company, OEM, hyperscaler, et cetera?...

Patrizio Vinciarelli

So I cannot disclose any of the details that you're looking for. I can only say that at the shareholders meeting that there's been no license in connection with this particular action. So you should not assume that the parties involved got a license and by mutual license, they are able to keep doing what may have been subject to exclusion order and potentially other actions were coming.

As you can probably guess from the QA above, the question that was asked was not about the Delta settlement, but about the hyperscaler licensing deal, because the infringer is not a "customer." So we have no clarity on who was the company that signed the $200 million deal, whether is it recurring, and so on.

Competitive Reality

The other, broader problem is that Vicor is not doing too well as a business. Ignoring one-time monies, revenue has been poor - for example, the $359 million they earned last year was an 11.4% drop from the year before that, and 2023 net revenue was just 1.5% above 2022. The key reason? Vicor’s FPA architecture has been overshadowed by multi-phase regulator technologies from the likes of Monolithic Power Systems, Inc. (MPWR), which are fractionally less efficient in terms of power conversion but much less expensive.

The difference is in design philosophy. Companies like MPWR, Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN), and Analog Devices, Inc. (ADI) use these multi-phase regulators that distribute regulation in a step-down manner, whereas FPA does a single voltage step-down right at the point of load. FPA allows much of the board to carry high-voltage, low-current power, reducing copper loss and improving density. The disadvantage is cost—Vicor's discrete modular design and proprietary packaging make it more expensive to produce than integrated silicon VRMs.

A related problem for end-users is that Vicor alone owns the tech for these highly power-dense and efficient NBMs, so very big companies who prefer to have a diverse supply chain might not want to get involved. That is one reason that NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and AMD switched to MPWR’s multi-phase design in their latest GPUs (NVDA’s H100, AMD MI300) despite FPA’s theoretical superiority with 48V. However, there is an emerging global trend of shifting from 12V to 48V because that can drastically reduce resistive (I²R) losses.

Background: Understanding the 12V-to-48V Transition

The 12-volt power distribution standard has been widely used across the electronic and computing systems industry. This was sufficient in most conditions at that time, when power demands per rack were modest. However, many modern applications require much higher power, so the current required at just 12V became extremely high. This causes energy waste through resistive (I²R) losses—energy dissipated as heat when electrical current (I) passes through the resistance (R') of conductors.

These losses grow with the square of current, so even modest increases in current cause disproportionately large efficiency losses. Shifting to 48 volts reduces the current needed for the same power by a factor of four, cutting resistive losses by roughly sixteen times (4²). This higher voltage also allows for thinner, lighter cables and more compact board layouts without compromising safety.

The good thing about 48V is that it still remains below the SELV (Safety Extra-Low Voltage) threshold of 60V. This is the upper safety limit defined by international electrical standards (IEC 60950/62368). This means 48V systems deliver major efficiency and density gains while staying within safe-to-touch voltage levels, making them ideal for data centers, automotive systems, and high-density electronics.

The 48-Volt Tailwind

Like I noted just now, 48V massively reduces I²R losses. The math is simple - current falls by 4x at 48 V, and losses scale with the square of current. Ordinarily, this would be too perfect, and MPWR would be “good enough,” but modern high-performance servers have extreme power demands per rack, which makes 12V systems impractical for wiring. In EV vehicles and other automotive systems, the same scalability benefit applies - 48V can allow for dramatically lighter wire harness:

…delivering 950W of 12V power (79A) across a 12ft. wiring harness requires a 4AWG cable that weighs roughly 2kg (4.1 lbs.). In contrast, powering the same 950W load with 48V can be accomplished using 10AWG wire, which weighs 0.24kg (>0.5 lb.), resulting in an 85% savings in weight (Figure 2).

48V is still well within the SELV threshold (<60 V), so it is a “sweet spot” between efficiency and safety. This has utility in many modern high-power demanding applications. And Vicor holds the IP to it - as those Taiwanese companies just found out.

The favorable legal outcomes have shown that a) what Vicor has is original and the patents are proven to be enforceable, and b) it is widely copied, i.e., there’s a large market for it. That last point has been even further bolstered by the new 48V trend. However, this has not converted to a decided change in the company's operational trajectory or bottomline.

That brings us to the valuation question: should we value this company for what it has done in the last few years, or should we consider what it can do with its reinforced IP moat and technical superiority? Vicor’s new ChiP (Converter-housed-in-Package) technology is FPA-based but is more easily assembled modules with standard SMT lines. Vicor’s trademark is its power density. But Vicor now needs mass manufacturability and cost parity with integrated multi-phase solutions. Can ChiP make that happen, giving Vicor an edge over larger rivals like Monolithic and Texas Instruments?

Valuation and Peer Comparison

Vicor’s current market cap is ~$2.3 billion. The stock has been volatile this year, going up and down—and the valuation seems conservative, with a price to sales ratio near 6X. Relatively speaking, however, TXN and ADI trade at 10-11X, while MPWR is currently at ~18X—but these companies have diversified portfolios or high profitability. MPWR, especially, has seen noteworthy numbers, growing an impressive 121% (EPS') in 2024, as it got design wins from Nvidia. It generated $2.21 billion in revenues last year, with 21% YoY growth.

Now, let us look at intrinsic valuation using two models: one, where the one-time IP/licensing money kicks in, but Vicor continues as before, and two, where Vicor takes strong advantage of the 48V trend and becomes an operational success.

Here are my inputs: Vicor had revenues of $359.1 million in 2024. Their Q2’25 cash & equivalents were $338.5 million. There was no financial debt on the balance sheet (only leases). Their shares outstanding were 45 million, with 33.14 million in common shares and 11.74 million in Class B shares. They booked the $45 million settlement in Q2, while management guided to ~$200 million “returns/licensing-related revenue” through ~Mar’26.

For both scenarios, I will assume core DCF settings as: WACC 11%, terminal growth 3%, tax 21%, D&A 5% of sales, CapEx 6–7% of sales, and change in Net Working Capital (ΔNWC) 1–2% of revenue growth. ΔNWC will be higher in the second scenario.

Now, for Scenario A, which has an IP focus, but flat growth otherwise, we will calculate for the period between 2025 and 2030. We reduce base/product/royalty revenue growth by 1% every time, so in 2025 we have $390 million, which grows 5% in 26-27, 4% in 28-29, and 3% in 2030. Operating margins on base revenue grow from 10% to 15% by 2030. Fees from IP are assumed to be $120 million in 2025, $80 million in 2026 (from management statement of ~$200 million during this period). This tapers down to $20 million per year in recurring royalties between 2027 and 2030.

Applying these revenue and margin assumptions to cash generation after tax gives me these free cash flow (FCF') outcomes (2025→2030):≈ $112 million, $88 million, $50 million, $56 million, $61 million, $67 million (each year 2025→2030). These numbers have been rounded up to account for small percentage differences in my inputs.

Now I'll do the DCF valuation. I get $318 million as the PV of explicit FCFs between 2025 and 2030. PV of terminal value is $458 million. That gives me an Enterprise value of $776 million. Given net cash of $339 million, I get an equity value of $1.15 billion. Dividing across ~45 million shares, I get a per share value of ~$24.78 in the first scenario.

Moving on to Scenario B, where I calculate an incremental value add through quality manufacturing execution between 2025 and 2030, we start with Scenario A, where we get an Equity value of $1.15 billion (~$25/share rounded). What we are essentially assuming is that Vicor retains IP revenue through scenario A, but strong execution and ChiP manufacturing incrementally add to it, does not replace it. Here we assume a higher net revenue growth, so starting at 2025 base revenue of $390 million, we get 20% growth in 2026, 18% in 2027, 15% in 2028, 12% in 2029, and 10% in 2030. That is $780 million in 2030.

We ramp up operating margin by 7 pts over Scenario A, so we have 12% at base ending at 22% in 2030. We front-loaded capex to 7% and 6% of sales, and ΔNWC we set at ~2% to reflect inventory and receivables.

From these assumptions, we get additional FCFs of $43 million, $54 million, $71 million, $92 million, $109 million, and $127 million during the years calculated. The first couple of years are slightly optimistic, but that averages out in the latter years. Combining these numbers with Scenario A, we get combined FCFs of $155 million, $142 million, $121 million, $148 million, $170 million, and $194 million.

Now, I calculate the DCF using 11% WACC and a 3% terminal growth rate. I get PV of explicit FCFs: $647 million (= $318 million A + $329 million B). PV of terminal value is $1.33 billion (= $458 million A + $872 million B). I get an enterprise value of $1.98 billion. Adding net cash, I get an equity value of $2.32 billion. That gives me a per share price of $54.

Vicor’s current share price is $49, so most of this is baked into the share price. Unless execution is near perfect, I would consider VICR to be trading at a premium.

Conclusion and Outlook

Vicor surely has great technology, as its patent win and licensing deals demonstrate. However, technology alone is not enough here, and larger rivals have gone way beyond Vicor’s reach with their inexpensive, “good enough” products that are scalable and adequately efficient. Vicor may well execute flawlessly on both licensing and ChiP, but it is not going to be able to capture meaningful share in the AI and data center markets, where having a diverse supply chain and cost per watt advantages outweigh technical superiority.

At $49 per share, much of Vicor’s future potential is baked into the stock. An investor buying it now will be paying a premium, unless Vicor executes flawlessly and consistently until the end of the decade, in which case there will be a small percentage of appreciation.

data.title: Northern Oil & Gas: Note Refinancing Pushes Next Note Maturity Until 2029
data.description:
Northern Oil & Gas (NYSE:NOG) recently refinanced its 8.125% unsecured notes due March 2028 with new 7.875% unsecured notes due October 2033. This makes Northern's next note maturity its 3.625% convertible notes due April 2029. I last covered Northern in September.

Northern's debt maturity situation looks good, although it will need to work on debt reduction to get its leverage down to its long-term 1.0x target.

Due to lower oil prices (and their effect on near-term free cash flow), I am reducing Northern's estimated value from $33 to $32 per share. I am maintaining a longer-term (after 2026) outlook for $70 WTI oil and believe that the weak near-term oil prices (if prolonged) would enhance the odds of $70+ oil in 2027.

Northern's hedging position is such that it won't benefit from oil going above $70 in 2026 (and could actually see massive hedging losses if oil goes well above $70 then). Thus it would prefer a situation where oil prices increase significantly in 2027 instead.

Debt Tender Offer

Northern had $705.1 million in outstanding 8.125% unsecured notes due in 2028. Holders of $684.9 million of those notes tendered their notes for Northern's offer of 101.5% of principal plus accrued and unpaid interest.

This was 97.1% of the total notes outstanding, so Northern will redeem the remaining 2.9% at a similar price. If less than 90% of the notes were tendered, then Northern would have waited until March 2026 to redeem the remaining notes at 100% of principal plus accrued and unpaid interest.

New Notes

Northern issued $725 million in 7.875% unsecured notes due 2033. The interest rate on the new notes is slightly lower than Northern's 2028 notes, but the annual interest costs will be approximately the same due to the $20 million in additional principal.

Northern's credit facility debt (net of cash) would also be roughly unchanged after the deal due to the slight premium with the tender offer and the costs associated with the new note offering.

Northern's Debt Situation

Northern had approximately $2.36 billion in net debt at the end of Q2 2025. It may end 2025 with around $2.3 billion in net debt at current strip prices and a $0.45 per share quarterly dividend, but without accounting for further share repurchases.

Northern's $700 million in convertible notes due April 2029 don't mature for several more years. However, those convertible notes have a conversion price of $36.87 per share, which is well above Northern's current share price.

Northern probably needs $70s oil for its share price to get above that level again. Otherwise it will be looking at refinancing those notes in several years, with interest rates that may be around 4 percentage points higher than the 3.625% rate on the convertible notes if it decides that it doesn't want to go the convertible route again.

Notes On Hedges

One thing to note is that Northern is betting on oil prices remaining relatively low in 2026 and thus may not benefit if oil prices rise beyond $70 in 2026 due to its short swaption position. Northern has a short swaption position involving 60,500 barrels per day of oil in 2026 at an average price of $69.90 per barrel.

Northern's 2026 oil hedges have around $72 million in value at $60 WTI oil, but negative $7 million in value at $70 WTI oil and negative $366 million at $80 WTI oil (if the counterparties exercise their options).

Northern also has some swaptions and short call options in 2027/2028, but those are much smaller in quantity.

My baseline scenario involves current strip prices until the end of 2026, followed by $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas after that.

That scenario would work out fine for Northern, as it would have close to $100 million in 2026 hedging gains, while its 2027 and 2028 short swaptions and short call options are at average prices above $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX gas.

Notes On Valuation

I am trimming my estimate of Northern's value from $33 per share to $32 per share. This is due to Q4 2025 and 2026 oil strip prices dropping below $60. Although Northern is partially hedged, this does still have some effect on its near-term free cash flow.

If sub-$60 oil remains for a while, Northern may need to reduce its capex budget and/or reduce its dividend. I don't believe that Northern can maintain both its value-weighted production and current dividend in a sub-$60 oil environment without cash burn.

A prolonged period of near-term sub-$60 oil would increase the odds of $70+ oil in 2027, though.

Conclusion

Northern Oil & Gas has dealt with its nearest note maturity, and its interest costs are roughly unchanged. Northern's leverage is somewhat higher than ideal, though, so it will need to work on debt reduction. At current strip prices for 2026, it may have to cut its capex and/or dividend to be able to make some headway with debt reduction next year, though.

I am reducing Northern's value slightly to $32 per share due to weaker near-term free cash flow projections, although I am maintaining an outlook for $70 WTI oil in 2027 and beyond. The near-term slump in oil prices should help improve the odds of $70+ WTI oil in 2027.

Northern would also prefer $70+ oil to happen in 2027 rather than sometime in 2026 due to the way its hedges are set up.

data.title: AMC: Massive Dilution Could Be Coming
data.description:
One of the hardest-hit companies during the coronavirus pandemic was AMC Entertainment (NYSE:AMC). The theater business struggled to stay afloat as moviegoer attendance plunged and numerous releases were delayed. The company used a series of debt and equity offerings to avoid bankruptcy over the years, but its financial situation is still rather ugly. This week, the company filed a proxy statement for its shareholder meeting, with one major ask of shareholders that could significantly hurt the stock.

Previous coverage of the name

I last covered the stock back in August after the company reported Q2 results, at which time I suggested investors sell the recent rally. While headline results beat Street estimates, as they almost always do, the balance sheet remained a major problem, and Q3 box office numbers so far had been sluggish. I discussed the possibility of management asking shareholders to approve an increase in the authorized share count so it could start selling equity again. Since that time, AMC shares have declined by more than 5.4%, while the S&P 500 has rallied more than 4.2%, and US markets have been setting a bunch of new highs in recent months.

A review of current financials

As a reminder, AMC finished Q2 with around $424 million in cash on the balance sheet. While total debt continues to come down over time, it still stood at more than $4 billion at the end of June, while shareholder's equity on the balance sheet remained at more than $1.725 billion in the red. The company did announce a debt deal in July to refinance some of its borrowings, so we'll see the full effect of those moves at the Q3 report. According to box office data, Q3 grosses were down 11% over the prior year period. A quarter in which AMC reported a cash burn of more than $92 million.

When AMC reported Q3 2024 results last November, the company announced total revenues of just under $1.35 billion. If you simply take this year's box office number for Q3 and prorate revenues against last year's period, it would put the company just under $1.20 billion for this year's period. That is roughly in the ballpark of the current street average of $1.22 billion. However, I should note that AMC has beaten analyst expectations four quarters in a row and in 11 of the last 12 periods, so I wouldn't be surprised if the end result comes in a little above the proration estimate.

In terms of other results, I'm a little more skeptical. In the first six months of this year, AMC reported 14% revenue growth, or about $278 million over the first half of 2024. However, the company's first half net loss increased by over $10 million from last year to this, and cash burn also increased by over $10 million. Given street revenue estimates call for a more than 9% top line decline for Q3, I would think we'll see a larger loss and cash burn than last year's $21 million and $92 million, respectively.

The share count could soar yet again

AMC now has over 512 million shares outstanding after the recent debt deal. As the chart below shows, shareholders who have held the stock since prior to the pandemic have been massively diluted over time, and yet the balance sheet is still in terrible shape. The company did catch a small break recently in that one of its debt deals was adjusted to save the company nearly $40 million, but that's a drop in the bucket at this point.

The company had an authorized common stock share count of 550 million, which it obviously was getting very close to the limit. Even before the debt deal was reached a few months back, I had been discussing the possibility of an increase. Management had previously stated that it would not sell any more shares without shareholder approval. Well, as the proxy linked in my opening detailed, proposal five at this year's shareholder meeting asks the following:

To approve an amendment to our Certificate of Incorporation to increase the total number of authorized shares of our Class A Common Stock, par value $0.01 per share (“Common Stock”) from 550,000,000 shares to 1,100,000,000 shares (“Proposal 5”).

The shareholder meeting will be on December 10th, which will be a month or so after Q3 results are reported. The problem is that even if AMC sold another 500 million shares at current levels, it wouldn't even raise $1.5 billion, meaning the net debt pile would still be around $2 billion or so. Also, if the company were to try and sell that many shares, it likely wouldn't get anywhere near that much in proceeds, given a doubling of the share count would likely put significant downward pressure on the stock.

Of course, there is always the chance that shareholders reject this proposal to double the share count. If this happens, it would provide limited flexibility for AMC to raise capital from equity. Thus, management would have to tap the debt markets further if it needs more funds, which would result in more interest expenses, losses, and cash burn. In this case, it's possible that AMC management could call a special shareholder meeting where it asks for approval to raise the share count to a lower number, say 800 million. It might even be possible to see a new tiered vote that asks for approval in, say, 100 million share increments, to see how much dilution shareholders are willing to accept.

A look at current valuations

As of Thursday's close, AMC shares were trading for about 0.30 times their expected revenue for this year. Competitor Cinemark Holdings, Inc. (CNK) trades for about 1.23 times its respective 2025 revenue estimate while being projected for a little less top line growth in percentage terms. Currently, Cinemark has a bit of debt as well, but it also has positive working capital, GAAP profitability, and positive free cash flow. As a result, it's not a surprise to see AMC shares trade at such a large discount given its significantly distressed financial situation.

When it comes to street analysts, there is a bit of a mixed picture here. 5 of the 7 covering AMC shares have a hold, with one analyst having a strong buy and another analyst having a strong sell. The average price target of $3.34 represents about 14% upside from current levels. However, that average valuation was nearly $4.65 a year ago and almost $35 (split-adjusted) three years ago, so analysts have been cutting their targets significantly over time.

When it comes to the bull case, there is the hope that the theater business improves through the end of 2026. Analysts are expecting 12.7% revenue growth in Q4 2025 and nearly 7% total growth for 2026, based on a strong expected film slate. If AMC can get its top line to grow as expected, it hopefully can reduce losses and cash burn. This would allow it to potentially refinance some of its high interest borrowings, further improving the financial picture. Selling a bit more equity to help with the debt pile would certainly help the long term financials, albeit at a short term hit to shareholders.

Final thoughts and recommendation

AMC's annual proxy filing came out this week, and management is asking shareholders to double the amount of authorized common shares. With the company still in a more than $3 billion net debt hole, equity sales are likely the best way to improve the balance sheet from here. Unfortunately, the massive drop in shares in recent years means shareholders will be significantly diluted if equity sales resume, even if only to raise a few hundred million dollars.

With the Q3 box office numbers not looking good and the potential for massive dilution again on the horizon, I am continuing to rate AMC shares as a sell today. The company is not going to significantly improve its balance sheet without raising fresh capital, and investors are likely going to pay the price as a result. While the stock is already down more than 92% over the past five years, there could be even more pressure into 2026 should shareholders approve the proposal and management start selling equity again.

data.title: Alibaba's Great Ride Higher Will Be Slower From Here (Downgrade)
data.description:
Back in early July of this year, I decided to reaffirm e-commerce and cloud computing behemoth Alibaba Group Holding Limited (NYSE:BABA) as a "Buy" candidate. I argued that the stock remained heavily discounted, even after factoring in that it is a Chinese company. The business has seen some mixed results in recent years. But in key areas, it has captured rapid growth. My timing was almost flawless. I say this because, since reaffirming it as a "Buy" candidate, the stock has risen 48%. This comes at a time when the S&P 500 is up only 4%. And since I originally rated it a "Buy" in March 2023, shares have risen 72% compared to the 64% rise that the S&P 500 enjoyed.

This surge in price can be attributed to the market finally realizing that the company was undervalued. But the real catalyst was the revelation that AI and cloud computing will continue to yield significant opportunities for shareholders. This does serve as a massive market opportunity that will shape the global economy. However, there can only be so much market-beating upside. And while the company is still trading at a discount compared to other similar firms, I would argue that it's close enough, especially given its Chinese origins, to justify a downgrade to a "Hold."

Time to be cautious

Fundamentally speaking, the picture for Alibaba has been somewhat mixed but has definitely tilted toward the positive. Take the most recent quarter as an example, which would be the first quarter of the company’s 2026 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the company came in at $34.57 billion. That does represent an increase over the $33.47 billion that the company reported the previous year. As revenue grew, profitability jumped also, soaring from $3.34 billion to $6.02 billion. But other profitability metrics have not been as great. Adjusted net income, for instance, contracted from $5.60 billion to $4.68 billion. Operating cash flow declined from $4.63 billion to $2.89 billion. And EBITDA fell from $7.04 billion to $6.38 billion.

The best way to understand what is going on here is to dig into each of its operating segments. In the past, the company had several segments. But today, it only has four, as it has sought to simplify its operations. The largest of these is none other than its Alibaba China E-commerce Group, which focuses on the e-commerce operations of the business in its home market. This includes providing customers with food, groceries, electronics, apparel, and other products in the same way that Amazon (AMZN) does.

During the most recent quarter, revenue for the business came in at $19.55 billion. That's an increase of 11.3% over the $17.57 billion that the company reported the previous year. A big driver of this growth was the 10% rise in customer management revenue that the company achieved, driven by an increase in take rate as the enterprise added additional software service fees in September of last year. Additional growth associated with its Quanzhantui platform, its AI-powered digital marketing and advertising tool made available for merchants on its Taobao and Tmall platforms, also helped to push sales higher.

Another big growth driver of the company seems to be its 88VIP membership program that focuses on further engaging and monetizing high-value customers that the company has. Since its launch in August 2018, 88VIP has achieved rapid growth. By the second quarter of this year, it hit 53 million members. That's up from the 50 million reported for the first quarter of the year and translates to a 26.2% jump compared to the 42 million that the company had in the second quarter of 2025. According to management, this is an area where the company continues to focus on not only user growth but retention as well, as the business makes additional investments aimed at increasing the value proposition for its customers. Seeing as how these are the highest value customers because they are also the highest spenders, this is a great strategy if you ask me.

I wish I could say that bottom-line results for this segment were doing better. Profitability actually fell from $6.71 billion to $5.34 billion. The good news is that this was not because the segment itself is struggling. Rather, it's because management continues to make big investments in operations aimed at achieving long-term growth and profitability. This includes investments in improving user experiences, acquiring additional customers, and utilizing technology in other key ways.

This is not the only area of growth that the company saw. For instance, the Alibaba International Digital Commerce Group, which focuses on its international e-commerce operations, saw revenue expand from $4.03 billion to $4.85 billion. This increase was driven by a surge in international commerce retail demand, with a lot of this attributable to its AliExpress and Trendyol platforms. Management did not give a breakdown of how much each of these grew. But they did say that their international commerce retail business expanded by 20% year-over-year. We also know that on the wholesale side, revenue grew by roughly 13% because of additional cross-border-related value-added services.

On the bottom line, we did see some nice improvements here. The firm went from a loss of $510 million the previous year to a loss of only $8 million this year. Management said that AliExpress reported meaningful improvements in operating efficiency. There were also improvements when it came to other platforms such as Alibaba.com, Lazada, and Trendyol. Management continues to make investments here in AI-powered tools that are used for things like marketing, procurement, and product listing. This should, over the long run, only improve the value proposition of the business.

The last meaningful segment that we have is my favorite. And that happens to be the Cloud Intelligence Group, which, as its name suggests, focuses on cloud computing and a lot of the AI-related activity. Management saw revenue skyrocket from $3.65 billion to $4.66 billion. It appears as though this can be chalked up mostly to public cloud revenue growth as customers continue to adopt AI-related offerings for their own operations. In fact, according to management, AI-related product revenue achieved triple-digit growth on a year-over-year basis, making it the 8th consecutive quarter where this was the case. Management continues to launch additional LLMs, which are also likely fueling optimism in the business, as is the fact that in February of this year, the firm announced it would be spending $52 billion in AI and cloud infrastructure over the next three years.

As the top line for this segment improved, the bottom line improved also. Profitability grew from $322 million to $412 million. Even though the company has invested heavily in this part of the business, the growth on the public cloud side and improvements in operating efficiencies have more than offset this. This is great to see, and it mirrors many of the other major cloud computing companies that I have analyzed over the last few years now. As they grow, bottom-line results improve at a nice pace.

There is another part of the company. And this is its All Others business. This is basically a collection of other units that on their own don't justify having their own segment designations. Examples here include Cainiao, Freshippo, Alibaba Health, and Amap. The last of these, Amap, is particularly interesting to me. On Oct. 2 of this year, news broke that this platform, which is an AI mapping tool, now has over 360 million daily active users. This is evidence that it brings meaningful value to its users. Unfortunately, overall revenue for this segment did drop from $11.19 billion to $8.15 billion. But this was because of the firm's decision to sell off some of its operations and because of some weakness associated with Cainiao. Given the magnitude of this decline, we should probably be happy that profitability declined only slightly from negative $148 million to negative $198 million.

In all likelihood, we will continue to see some pressure on the bottom line this year thanks to big investments that management is making. But honestly, that's a case of short-term pain in exchange for long-term gain. Unfortunately, this does still negatively affect the valuation of the business today. If we project out results based on what we have seen already for the year, we would expect adjusted net income to come in at around $18.21 billion. Operating cash flow would be about $18.83 billion, while EBITDA would amount to $25.28 billion.

Using these estimates and the historical results the company achieved last year, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently valued. These are not awful multiples. In fact, as the chart below illustrates, the firm is trading cheaper than two major U.S. players that I think are comparable to it. But as I detailed in a previous article about Alibaba some time ago, there are legitimate reasons to discount it compared to its Western competitors even though the business remains in a prime position to benefit from massive AI growth in China.

Takeaway

At the end of the day, I would make the case that while Alibaba is far from a bad opportunity, it's no longer as attractive as it once was. I think that the company is much closer to being a fair value play than it is anything else, given the totality of the circumstances. Because of this, and because I am in general cautious about the market right now, I believe that downgrading it to a "Hold" is the right choice.

data.title: ADT: The IPO Of Verisure Highlights The Potential For Upside
data.description:
In my first article about ADT (NYSE:ADT), a home security company, the investment thesis was built around a handful of elements. ADT is a transformed and more focused company; it has powerful partnerships and owners, and it is consistently reducing its leverage. The stock price has been pressured by the sales of shares of private equity investor Apollo, which is still the largest owner. The valuation of ADT is low considering the recent and projected revenue and earnings growth and quality of the business.

Previously there has been no comparable peer for ADT. In the beginning of October, a company called Verisure listed on the Stockholm stock exchange. It was the largest IPO in Europe since 2022. ADT and Verisure are very similar. The biggest difference between the two companies is their respective geographical coverage. ADT operates only in the U.S., while Verisure is active in Europe and South America. Now, with a peer in sight, we can say that either ADT is undervalued, Verisure is overvalued, or the truth is somewhere in between.

Most of the figures are heading in the right direction

ADT reported its Q2 results at the end of July. The company delivered a 7% revenue increase, and earnings per share jumped 35%. Installation revenue was $197 million, which increased 43% thanks to the sales of ADT+ equipment. Here, the impact of tariffs can cause weakness on both top and bottom lines. As a result of good business performance, lower SG&A expenses (mainly advertising and administration), and lower share count, ADT increased the EPS guidance for 2025 to $0.81-0.89 per share.

Churn is still a major challenge

One part of the investment thesis was and is ADT’s partnerships with Google (GOOGL) and State Farm, which are both significant owners of ADT. State Farm was supposed to be a new complementary sales channel for ADT. However, the sales have progressed slower than expected. Through State Farm, ADT has only added 33,000 subscribers, as highlighted by the management in the latest earnings call. ADT+ service appears to be rolling out very well, based on the high increase in installation revenue, which should support recurring revenue growth too.

In Q2, ADT’s leverage ratio decreased to 2.8x. However, this was due to higher EBITDA, and ADT’s net debt remained constant. Before the IPO, Verisure’s indebtedness was significantly higher at 4.5x. One of the intended uses of the raised capital is to reduce the debt load down to a leverage ratio of 3x.

One of the greatest challenges of ADT is the high churn of customers. At the end of Q2, attrition was 12.8%. There's been no improvement in the past year, although it has slowly inched lower. The number of subscribers also appears to remain static, even if ADT repeatedly acquires bulks of customer portfolios. In Q2, ADT acquired 50,000 subscribers for $89 million. It spends around $1.4 billion in customer acquisition just to remain flat.

ADT is pursuing utilizing remote assistance and AI in order to serve its customers better and more efficiently. An investor should carefully monitor the progress here. Of course, customer service should be better when routine tasks are handled automatically and remotely. The partnership with Google, offering Google’s hardware, will only help over time. Selling wider smart home solutions supports revenue growth and customer lock-in. ADT’s attrition is significantly higher than Verisure’s. One potential reason could be that Verisure uses Arlo’s hardware.

ADT is still cheap compared to Verisure

ADT shares have risen 28% year to date. Due to increasing earnings, the multiple has nearly remained constant. Currently, ADT trades at a forward P/E multiple of 10x. Analysts expect ADT to deliver double-digit earnings growth for the couple of following years. Of course that won’t continue for long, but a 10x P/E multiple does not take into account the growth and other qualities of the business, such as a high share of recurring revenue, strong cash flow generation, and high profitability.

Verisure attained a much richer valuation in its debut on the Stockholm bourse. Some of the factors justifying higher valuation are faster growth (around revenue growth 10% in average), significantly lower churn, and a cleaner track record. It has not done similar strategic missteps as ADT in the past. In much of Europe and South America, Verisure has significant potential for growth as the penetration of home security services increases.

On the other hand, Verisure is smaller, and it has much higher debt and lower profitability. Therefore, paying two to three times more for Verisure seems like a stretch. And the valuation mismatch appears to be expanding as Verisure shares are heading upwards in the first days of listing. Figures in the table below are calculated on the basis of listing price.
